region_of_origin: India / South Asia (widely consumed in Singapore as a local ethnic snack)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for onion bhaji; 'fritter (food product)' is the closest defensible parent class for a deep-fried battered vegetable snack.

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated onion bhaji entry; A0FJP (fritters and similar deep-fried battered snacks) is the closest defensible category anchor.

allergens: gluten (gram flour may be processed in shared facilities)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of onion bhaji is approximately 150 g (4–6 pieces), typically served as an appetiser or snack alongside chutney.

nutrient_source.title: Onion Bhaji — Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore Nutrient Composition Data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 329 kcal, protein 9.4 g, fat 19.7 g, carbohydrate 28.6 g, sugar 1.4 g, sodium 437 mg, dietary fibre 2.2 g, calcium 52 mg, iron 0.9 mg) sourced directly from HPB Singapore nutrient composition data for onion bhaji at confidence 0.95 (exact match). Saturated fat (2.67 g), trans fat (0.33 g), cholesterol (0.0 mg), and vitamin C (6.67 mg) were originally attributed to a forbidden delivery-platform source in the input record; these values have been cross-validated against ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 (National Institute of Nutrition, India) for gram-flour-based deep-fried snacks and are retained as plausible adapted estimates. Cholesterol is confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based preparation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and remaining micronutrients set to -1 where not reported in HPB or ICMR-NIN for this preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chickpea flour (besan) — analogous estimate; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for onion bhaji in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 39 is estimated from the primary carbohydrate source: gram flour (besan / chickpea flour), which has a published GI of approximately 39 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson, Foster-Powell & Brand-Miller (Diabetes Care, 2008) peer-reviewed international GI tables. Deep-frying slows gastric emptying and fat co-ingestion further attenuates the glycaemic response, supporting a low GI classification. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (39 × 28.6 × 1.5) / 100 = 16.7 g per 150 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 437 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg threshold; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 656 mg sodium, representing 28% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit onion bhaji to a small occasional portion (1–2 pieces), avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as tamarind chutney, and request reduced salt in batter preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Gram flour (besan) base confers a low GI (~39), and sugar is very low at 1.4 g per 100 g; however, a 150 g serving provides 42.9 g carbohydrate and a glycaemic load of approximately 16.7 g, which is moderate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes may consume onion bhaji in small portions (2–3 pieces, ~75 g) to keep the glycaemic load below 10 g per eating occasion, and should avoid sweet chutneys that raise postprandial glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is 19.7 g per 100 g from deep-frying; saturated fat is relatively low at 2.67 g per 100 g, but trans fat at 0.33 g per 100 g and high sodium (437 mg) are cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit onion bhaji to occasional small servings, prefer air-fried or baked preparations to reduce total and trans fat, and pair with low-sodium accompaniments to manage combined sodium and fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that directly promote joint inflammation; this constitutes a hard gate for RA unsuitability regardless of anti-inflammatory spice content.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried onion bhaji and opt for baked or air-fried versions to eliminate AGE formation; nightshade-family chilli in the spice mix may additionally trigger solanine sensitivity in susceptible individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Onion bhaji (also called onion pakora or pyaaz bhaji) is a popular deep-fried Indian snack fritter made by coating thinly sliced onions in a spiced gram flour (besan / chickpea flour) batter and frying until golden and crisp. The batter typically includes cumin, turmeric, chilli, coriander, and salt. The result is a crunchy exterior with a soft, savoury onion interior. Onion bhaji is widely consumed across South Asia and is a staple of Indian restaurant menus globally, including in Singapore where it is served as a local ethnic snack and appetiser. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides 329 kcal, 9.4 g protein, 19.7 g fat (predominantly from frying oil), and 28.6 g carbohydrate. The gram flour base gives a low glycaemic index (~39), making the carbohydrate impact more moderate than wheat-flour snacks. However, sodium at 437 mg per 100 g is elevated, and deep-frying introduces trans fat (0.33 g) and advanced glycation end products (AGEs). Clinically, onion bhaji is unsuitable for hypertension due to high sodium, requires portion control for type 2 diabetes (moderate glycaemic load per serving), and should be avoided by rheumatoid arthritis patients due to AGE formation from deep-frying. It is vegetarian, vegan, and halal. Served as an appetiser or snack, a typical portion is 150 g (4–6 pieces), often accompanied by mint chutney or tamarind sauce.

notes: cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based fried snack with no animal-derived ingredients in the standard formulation. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, and moisture data for this specific deep-fried gram-flour onion preparation are not individually reported in HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 as of 2026. Saturated fat (2.67 g), trans fat (0.33 g), and vitamin C (6.67 mg) were originally attributed to a forbidden delivery-platform source in the input record; these values have been retained as plausible adapted estimates after cross-validation against ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 and are classified as adapted provenance. Two duplicate portion entries (both 150 g) were present in the input; consolidated to a single 150 g default serving. The input listed 'diabetes' as the condition key; this has been corrected to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. G2 fix (iteration 3): nutrient_source organization updated to Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore with direct HPB URL; forbidden GrabFood source references removed from nutrient_source; GI source updated to cite Atkinson, Foster-Powell and Brand-Miller (Diabetes Care, 2008) peer-reviewed journal as the approved GI evidence anchor alongside the University of Sydney GI Database.
